title: He Ren, PhD, CFA
text: I’m an Assistant Professor of Finance at Merrilee Alexander Kick College of Business and Entrepreneurship, Texas Woman’s University. My research interest focuses on investments, household finance, and the impact of AI and Fintech on financial decision-making and markets.

title: Time Value of Money on the BA II Plus and in Excel
text:
Most introductory finance problems can be cracked two ways: on a TI BA II Plus financial calculator or in a spreadsheet. The calculator is what you bring to an exam; Excel is what you use at work. They give the same answer, but the workflow is different — the calculator stores one problem in memory, while Excel makes inputs visible and easy to tweak.
This post is a practical walkthrough for an undergraduate finance student. We start with calculator setup, move through the core TVM problems, and finish with NPV and IRR for uneven cash flows. Every example shows both the calculator keystrokes and the Excel formula.

title: Time Value of Money on the BA II Plus and in Excel
text:
Most introductory finance problems can be cracked two ways: on a TI BA II Plus financial calculator or in a spreadsheet. The calculator is what you bring to an exam; Excel is what you use at work. They give the same answer, but the workflow is different — the calculator stores one problem in memory, while Excel makes inputs visible and easy to tweak.
This post is a practical walkthrough for an undergraduate finance student. We start with calculator setup, move through the core TVM problems, and finish with NPV and IRR for uneven cash flows. Every example shows both the calculator keystrokes and the Excel formula.

title: Time Value of Money on the BA II Plus and in Excel
section: Setting Up the BA II Plus
text:
Setting Up the BA II Plus
Out of the box, the BA II Plus has two defaults that trip up students. Fix them once.
1. Set decimal places to 4 (or more). Press 2nd → FORMAT → enter 4 → ENTER → 2nd → QUIT.
2. Set payments per year to 1. Press 2nd → P/Y → enter 1 → ENTER → 2nd → QUIT. This is the safer default. When you have monthly compounding, you can either change P/Y to 12 or leave P/Y at 1 and enter the periodic rate manually (e.g., 0.5 for 6% / 12). I recommend the second approach — fewer hidden settings to forget.
3. Confirm END mode (not BGN). Most problems use end-of-period cash flows. Press 2nd → BGN. If it says END, press 2nd → QUIT. If it says BGN, press 2nd → SET → 2nd → QUIT.
4. Clear the TVM registers before every new problem. Press 2nd → CLR TVM. The calculator remembers your last problem; failing to clear is the #1 source of wrong answers on exams.






TipSign convention



The BA II Plus uses cash flow signs. Money you pay out is negative; money you receive is positive. If PV and FV have the same sign, the calculator returns an error. This convention matches Excel.

title: Time Value of Money on the BA II Plus and in Excel
text:
The Five TVM Keys
Every basic time value problem uses some subset of five variables, four of which you know and one you solve for.








Key
Meaning
Excel argument




N
Number of periods
nper


I/Y
Interest rate per period (as a percent)
rate


PV
Present value (lump sum today)
pv


PMT
Recurring payment each period
pmt


FV
Future value (lump sum at the end)
fv



One pitfall: in the BA II Plus, you enter I/Y as a percent (5 means 5%), but Excel’s rate argument wants a decimal (0.05). Forgetting this is the #2 source of wrong answers.
The next four examples walk through solving for each of the lump-sum unknowns — FV, PV, N, and I/Y — one at a time.

Example 1 — Solving for FV (future value of a lump sum)

You deposit $1,000 today in an account earning 5% per year. What is the balance in 10 years?

BA II Plus:



Step
Keystrokes
Display




1
2nd CLR TVM
0


2
10 N
N = 10


3
5 I/Y
I/Y = 5


4
1000 +/− PV
PV = −1,000


5
0 PMT
PMT = 0


6
CPT FV
FV = 1,628.89



Excel: =FV(0.05, 10, 0, -1000) → 1,628.89


Example 2 — Solving for PV (present value of a lump sum)

You’d like to have $50,000 saved for a down payment in 5 years. Your savings earn 6% per year. How much do you need to deposit today?

BA II Plus:



Step
Keystrokes
Display




1
2nd CLR TVM
0


2
5 N
N = 5


3
6 I/Y
I/Y = 6


4
0 PMT
PMT = 0


5
50000 FV
FV = 50,000


6
CPT PV
PV = −37,362.91



The negative sign means it’s an outflow today — you pay $37,362.91 now to receive $50,000 in five years.
Excel: =PV(0.06, 5, 0, 50000) → −37,362.91


Example 3 — Solving for N (how long?)

You invest $5,000 today at 8% per year. How many years until it doubles to $10,000?

BA II Plus:



Step
Keystrokes
Display




1
2nd CLR TVM
0


2
8 I/Y
I/Y = 8


3
5000 +/− PV
PV = −5,000


4
0 PMT
PMT = 0


5
10000 FV
FV = 10,000


6
CPT N
N = 9.0065



So it takes a little over nine years. (This is a quick check on the “Rule of 72”: 72 / 8 = 9 years, which is close.)
Excel: =NPER(0.08, 0, -5000, 10000) → 9.0065


Example 4 — Solving for I/Y (what rate did I earn?)

A friend tells you they bought a stock for $1,000 six years ago and just sold it for $1,500. What annualized rate of return did they earn?

BA II Plus:



Step
Keystrokes
Display




1
2nd CLR TVM
0


2
6 N
N = 6


3
1000 +/− PV
PV = −1,000


4
0 PMT
PMT = 0


5
1500 FV
FV = 1,500


6
CPT I/Y
I/Y = 6.9913



Roughly 6.99% per year.
Excel: =RATE(6, 0, -1000, 1500) → 0.069913


Example 5 — Future value of an annuity (monthly contributions)
Now add PMT to the mix.

You save $300 at the end of every month for 30 years in an account earning 7% APR compounded monthly. What’s the balance at the end?

The trick: convert annual quantities to monthly before you enter them. Periodic rate = 7% / 12 ≈ 0.5833%. Number of periods = 30 × 12 = 360.
BA II Plus:



Step
Keystrokes
Display




1
2nd CLR TVM
0


2
360 N
N = 360


3
7 ÷ 12 = I/Y
I/Y = 0.5833


4
0 PV
PV = 0


5
300 +/− PMT
PMT = −300


6
CPT FV
FV = 365,990.62



Excel: =FV(0.07/12, 360, -300, 0) → 365,990.62

title: Time Value of Money on the BA II Plus and in Excel
text:
APR vs EAR
The annual percentage rate (APR) is the stated, nominal rate. The effective annual rate (EAR) is what you actually earn or pay once compounding is accounted for. For any compounding frequency above 1, EAR > APR.

Example 6 — Converting APR to EAR

A credit card charges 18% APR compounded monthly. What’s the EAR?

BA II Plus: the ICONV worksheet does this in three keystrokes.



Step
Keystrokes
Display




1
2nd ICONV
NOM =


2
18 ENTER ↓
C/Y =


3
12 ENTER ↑ CPT
EFF = 19.5618



Excel: =EFFECT(0.18, 12) → 0.195618
Going the other way — from EAR to APR — use NOMINAL: =NOMINAL(0.195618, 12) → 0.18

title: Time Value of Money on the BA II Plus and in Excel
text:
Mortgages
A mortgage is just a present-value annuity problem in disguise. The bank lends you the present value; you repay with level monthly payments that include both principal and interest.

Example 7 — Monthly payment

You borrow $400,000 on a 30-year mortgage at 6.5% APR (compounded monthly). What’s the monthly payment?

BA II Plus:



Step
Keystrokes
Display




1
2nd CLR TVM
0


2
360 N
N = 360


3
6.5 ÷ 12 = I/Y
I/Y = 0.5417


4
400000 PV
PV = 400,000


5
0 FV
FV = 0


6
CPT PMT
PMT = −2,528.27



The negative sign means you’re paying out each month.
Excel: =PMT(0.065/12, 360, 400000) → −2,528.27
Each monthly payment is split between interest (charged on the outstanding balance) and principal (which reduces that balance). Early on, the balance is huge, so almost all of the payment is interest. Late in the loan, the balance is small, and most of the payment goes to principal. Excel exposes this split directly: IPMT returns the interest portion of a given payment and PPMT returns the principal portion. For month 1 of this loan:

=IPMT(0.065/12, 1, 360, 400000) → −2,166.67
=PPMT(0.065/12, 1, 360, 400000) → −361.60

Together they sum to the full payment. By month 360, the proportions are reversed.

title: Time Value of Money on the BA II Plus and in Excel
text:
Uneven Cash Flows: NPV
When the cash flows aren’t level, the basic TVM keys aren’t enough — you discount each cash flow on its own and add them up. On the BA II Plus, this is the CF (cash flow) worksheet.

Example 8 — NPV of a project

A project costs $10,000 today. It pays $2,000 at the end of year 1, $3,000 in year 2, $4,000 in year 3, and $5,000 in year 4. The discount rate is 10%. What’s the NPV?

BA II Plus: the CF worksheet stores cash flows by period.



Step
Keystrokes
Display




1
CF 2nd CLR WORK
CF0 = 0


2
10000 +/− ENTER ↓
CF0 = −10,000


3
2000 ENTER ↓ ↓
C01 = 2,000


4
3000 ENTER ↓ ↓
C02 = 3,000


5
4000 ENTER ↓ ↓
C03 = 4,000


6
5000 ENTER ↓ ↓
C04 = 5,000


7
NPV 10 ENTER ↓ CPT
NPV = 717.85



The ↓ ↓ between cash flows skips past the “frequency” prompt (F01, F02, …). If you have the same cash flow repeated several years in a row, you can enter the value once and set the frequency instead of typing it multiple times.
Excel: Excel’s NPV function discounts the first cash flow back one period, so you have to add the time-zero outflow separately.
=NPV(0.10, 2000, 3000, 4000, 5000) - 10000 → 717.85






WarningThe =NPV gotcha



Excel’s NPV assumes the first argument is one period away. The time-zero cash flow goes outside the function. Many wrong answers come from including -10000 inside the NPV parentheses.

title: Time Value of Money on the BA II Plus and in Excel
text:
IRR
The internal rate of return is the discount rate that makes NPV exactly zero. It’s the project’s break-even rate.

Example 9 — IRR of the same project
BA II Plus: with the cash flows from Example 8 still loaded, just press:
IRR CPT → IRR = 12.8258
Excel: put the cash flows in a column (including the −10,000 at time zero) and call:
=IRR(A1:A5) → 0.128258
Because the IRR (12.83%) is greater than the 10% discount rate used in Example 8, the project’s NPV is positive — exactly what we found. In general, accept a project when IRR > the required rate of return.






NoteWhen IRR misleads



IRR can have multiple solutions when cash flows change sign more than once (e.g., −, +, +, −). For mutually exclusive projects, IRR can also rank projects differently than NPV. NPV is the safer decision rule; IRR is the more intuitive one to communicate.

title: Time Value of Money on the BA II Plus and in Excel
text:
Cheat Sheet








Task
BA II Plus
Excel




Future value of lump sum
N I/Y PV 0 PMT CPT FV
=FV(rate, nper, 0, -pv)


Present value of lump sum
N I/Y FV 0 PMT CPT PV
=PV(rate, nper, 0, -fv)


Number of periods
I/Y PV PMT FV CPT N
=NPER(rate, pmt, pv, fv)


Periodic rate
N PV PMT FV CPT I/Y
=RATE(nper, pmt, pv, fv)


Annuity payment
N I/Y PV FV CPT PMT
=PMT(rate, nper, pv, fv)


APR → EAR
2nd ICONV, enter NOM and C/Y, CPT EFF
=EFFECT(apr, m)


EAR → APR
2nd ICONV, enter EFF and C/Y, CPT NOM
=NOMINAL(ear, m)


NPV (uneven cash flows)
CF worksheet, then NPV rate ENTER ↓ CPT
=NPV(rate, cf1:cfn) + cf0


IRR
CF worksheet, then IRR CPT
=IRR(cf0:cfn)


Interest portion of payment
(Calculate manually from balance × rate)
=IPMT(rate, period, nper, pv)


Principal portion of payment
(Payment minus interest portion)
=PPMT(rate, period, nper, pv)

title: Time Value of Money on the BA II Plus and in Excel
text:
Closing Thoughts
Three habits will save you in this class:

Always clear the calculator (2nd CLR TVM for TVM keys, 2nd CLR WORK inside CF) before starting a new problem.
Match the period. If the problem is monthly, your N, I/Y, and PMT all need to be monthly. Mixing annual and monthly is the most common error.
Watch the signs. If you put money in (a loan, an investment), it’s negative. If you take money out (a withdrawal, a repayment), it’s positive. Excel and the BA II Plus follow the same rule.

When you graduate, you’ll do almost everything in Excel — once a problem is bigger than the five TVM keys, the spreadsheet wins. But knowing both makes you faster, and it gives you a sanity check: if the calculator and the spreadsheet disagree, you’ve made a setup mistake somewhere, and you’ll catch it before turning it in.

title: Summary: Large Datasets and Structured Databases
text:
A summary of Paul Goldsmith-Pinkham’s article, published April 5, 2026.
Paul Goldsmith-Pinkham argues that AI coding agents (specifically Claude Code) have collapsed the fixed costs of professional data engineering, making it feasible for economists to build harmonized, documented, queryable databases from massive government datasets without specialized data engineering training.

title: Summary: Large Datasets and Structured Databases
text:
A summary of Paul Goldsmith-Pinkham’s article, published April 5, 2026.
Paul Goldsmith-Pinkham argues that AI coding agents (specifically Claude Code) have collapsed the fixed costs of professional data engineering, making it feasible for economists to build harmonized, documented, queryable databases from massive government datasets without specialized data engineering training.

title: Summary: Large Datasets and Structured Databases
text:
The Core Problem
Economists traditionally work with flat CSV files and copies because learning proper data infrastructure (parquet files, databases, schemas) required significant upfront investment. This made messy workflows a rational choice — the fixed cost of “doing it right” was too high to justify for one-off projects.

title: Summary: Large Datasets and Structured Databases
text:
The Demonstration
To prove the point, the author transformed a 70GB HMDA (Home Mortgage Disclosure Act) mortgage dataset — 291 million rows across 18 years — into a professional-grade pipeline using Claude Code. Work that traditionally takes weeks now takes hours.

Key results

Downloaded and harmonized 18 years of mortgage data from two incompatible sources (2007–2017 vs. 2018–2024)
Achieved 15× compression converting CSVs to parquet (70GB → 6GB)
Built a DuckDB database with a queryable metadata table documenting every column
Created county-by-year aggregate tables (HHI concentration, denial rates, median loan amounts, etc.)
Classified lenders as fintech vs. traditional banks
Found fintech origination share grew from ~1.1% (2007) to a peak of 16.3% (2021), settling around 11% by 2024

title: Summary: Large Datasets and Structured Databases
text:
The Original Prompts

Initial comprehensive prompt

I want to build a county-level panel dataset of mortgage lending in the US from 2007 to 2024 using HMDA (Home Mortgage Disclosure Act) data. The goal is to study the geographic expansion of fintech mortgage lenders over time.
The data comes from two sources (I’ve verified both are accessible):

2007–2017: CFPB historic data at https://files.consumerfinance.gov/hmda-historic-loan-data/hmda_{YEAR}_nationwide_all-records_codes.zip. These are zipped CSVs with numeric codes and the old LAR column names.
2018–2024: CFPB Data Browser API at https://ffiec.cfpb.gov/v2/data-browser-api/view/nationwide/csv?years={YEAR}. These stream as CSV with 99 columns, string codes, and the new format (uses lei instead of respondent_id, different column names throughout).

Each year is millions of rows. The full panel is 250M+ rows. This will not fit in pandas.
Here’s what I’d like you to do:

Write a download script that pulls the full LAR files for 2007–2024 from the URLs above. Include resume capability in case downloads get interrupted. Use a proper User-Agent header.
Convert each year’s CSV to parquet for efficient storage.
Set up a DuckDB database. Create a metadata table that documents every column — name, description, type, valid values, which years it’s available. This metadata table is important: I want future sessions to be able to query it and immediately understand the dataset without me re-explaining anything.

Important: the pre-2017 and post-2018 formats are very different — different column names, different coding schemes, different variable availability. You’ll need to figure out a crosswalk and harmonize into a consistent schema.
Key variables I care about: year, lender ID, lender name, county FIPS, state FIPS, loan type, loan purpose, action taken (originated, denied, etc.), loan amount, applicant race, applicant income. Use DuckDB and parquet throughout — no pandas DataFrames for the full data.



Feasibility inquiry

I want to classify lenders as fintech or shadow banks versus traditional. Start with the lender classification from Fuster, Plosser, Schnabl and Vickery (2019). Tell me what it would take to do this task before you try anything complicated. Is this feasible? What might be missing?



Aggregate table request

I want to build an aggregate database: county by year, total originations, dollar value, number of active lenders, the HHI (Herfindahl-Hirschman Index) of lender market shares, the denial rate, and the median loan amount.



Visualization request

Please make a figure of the trend in fintech origination share over time in the dataset, using Kieran Healy stylings for plotting.

title: Summary: Large Datasets and Structured Databases
text:
Key Insights
Metadata as context engineering. The metadata table acts as a form of “context engineering for data” — it stores column descriptions, valid values, and availability information directly inside the database. Future sessions can query the documentation without the human having to re-explain.
Right tools for big data. When data exceeds RAM, parquet and DuckDB are the right tools — pandas is the wrong tool.
Plan before coding. For complex pipelines, asking the model to scope the task before implementation pays off (the feasibility prompt above is a good example).

title: Summary: Large Datasets and Structured Databases
text:
Conclusion
The author argues that AI-assisted data engineering has fundamentally shifted the economics of research infrastructure. The bottleneck for empirical work has moved from “can I build the infrastructure?” to “do I have a worthwhile research question?”
Key takeaways:

The fixed costs of professional data engineering have collapsed
Large public datasets are more accessible than commonly assumed
Embedding metadata in the database makes future work cheaper
Planning matters more than coding speed

title: He Ren, PhD, CFA
text:
Welcome
Welcome! I’m an Assistant Professor of Finance whose research focuses on investments, household finance, and the impact of AI and Fintech on financial decision-making and markets. Feel free to explore my website to learn more about my teaching and research.
Outside the classroom and my research, I’m also a Rubik’s cube solver. You can check out my World Cube Association (WCA) profile to see my official results. I’m also passionate about photography. You can browse some of my favorite shots in the Photography section of this website.
LEARN MORE ABOUT ME →

title: Summary: From EDGAR Filings to a Structured Database
text:
A summary of Paul Goldsmith-Pinkham’s article.
This follow-up to the earlier HMDA post walks through a complete, end-to-end research pipeline built with Claude Code: pulling 10-K filings from SEC EDGAR, parsing out the Risk Factors section, and loading the results into a queryable database. The motivating research question is whether trade-exposed U.S. firms ramped up tariff-related language in their risk disclosures after the 2025 tariff escalation. The broader methodological point is that a researcher can go from a raw internet source to an analyzable dataset in roughly half an hour.

title: Summary: From EDGAR Filings to a Structured Database
text:
A summary of Paul Goldsmith-Pinkham’s article.
This follow-up to the earlier HMDA post walks through a complete, end-to-end research pipeline built with Claude Code: pulling 10-K filings from SEC EDGAR, parsing out the Risk Factors section, and loading the results into a queryable database. The motivating research question is whether trade-exposed U.S. firms ramped up tariff-related language in their risk disclosures after the 2025 tariff escalation. The broader methodological point is that a researcher can go from a raw internet source to an analyzable dataset in roughly half an hour.

title: Summary: From EDGAR Filings to a Structured Database
text:
The Pipeline
The author builds the pipeline in phases, with Claude Code first entering “plan mode” to read EDGAR API documentation and propose an approach before writing any code. The phases:

Research and planning. Claude reads EDGAR docs and proposes a design.
Design clarification. Claude asks the researcher to choose between DuckDB and SQLite, between simple keyword search and LLM classification, and what User-Agent string to use.
CIK lookup. Claude resolves Central Index Keys for 29 of 30 target firms. Gap Inc. fails because its ticker had changed (GPS → GAP).
Filing index queries. Claude hits the EDGAR API for each firm’s 10-Ks from 2022 to 2025.
Download. Raw HTML filings are downloaded with caching so reruns avoid redundant network calls.
Parsing. BeautifulSoup extracts Item 1A (Risk Factors), with regex tweaks for filings that split “Item 1A” and “Risk Factors” across lines.
Storage. Parsed text is loaded into DuckDB, alongside tariff keyword counts and context snippets.
Quality reports. An extraction-quality report flags parsing failures, which is how the author catches the Honeywell and Intel regex issue.
Querying. SQL queries against DuckDB produce the descriptive results.

The final dataset covers 119 of 120 filings successfully parsed. Total code: roughly 480 lines of Python.

title: Summary: From EDGAR Filings to a Structured Database
text:
The Original Prompts

Initial prompt

I want to build a dataset of 10-K Risk Factors from SEC EDGAR to study how tariff-related risk disclosure changed 2022–2025. Here’s what I know about EDGAR:

Every company has a CIK (Central Index Key)
The EDGAR filing API is at https://efts.sec.gov/LATEST/
Rate limit: 10 requests/second, must include User-Agent header

I want 10-Ks for ~30 trade-exposed firms — manufacturing, retail, tech hardware. Caterpillar, Nike, Apple, Deere, Ford, 3M, Intel…



Execution

Run it for the full list. Let’s see how it goes.



Querying the database

Which firms mentioned tariffs the most?

title: Summary: From EDGAR Filings to a Structured Database
text:
Key Findings
The descriptive results aren’t the main point, but they validate the pipeline:

John Deere had the highest tariff mention count, rising from three to four mentions over the period.
The 2022–2025 sample produced 26–35 tariff-related paragraphs per year, compared to 25 total across 18 firms in a 2010 baseline.
Language shifted from generic phrases like “changes in trade policy” to specific references — Section 301, particular product categories, “liberation day.”
Walmart did not mention tariffs until its 2025 filing.
Retail firms mentioned tariffs less often than manufacturing or tech-hardware firms.

title: Summary: From EDGAR Filings to a Structured Database
text:
Lessons
Front-load the scraping context. Specifying rate limits, headers, and API structure up front saves debugging rounds later.
Let Claude ask design questions. Instead of pre-specifying every detail, give Claude the high-level task and let it surface design choices (DuckDB vs. SQLite, keyword search vs. classification). The researcher’s job is to make the calls.
Build resume capability in. Network failures are common. Caching and idempotent scripts make reruns cheap — the Gap Inc. ticker fix required only four new downloads in 43 seconds, not a full re-pull.
Generate extraction-quality reports. They are how subtle parsing bugs get caught before they corrupt months of downstream analysis.
SQL beats CSVs for text-as-data. DuckDB gives zero-config deployment, SQL queryability, and relational structure without running a server.

title: Summary: From EDGAR Filings to a Structured Database
text:
Conclusion
The pipeline is small — one researcher, one afternoon, 480 lines of Python — but it shows how the per-project cost of building real research infrastructure has dropped. The interesting question is no longer whether the pipeline is feasible, but which research questions are worth pointing it at.
